name: Claude Opus 4.7
summary: Anthropic's best generally available model — 3x higher vision resolution (3.75MP), 1M token context, new 'xhigh' effort level, 64% on SWE-Bench Pro; same pricing as Opus 4.6

name: Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS
summary: expressive text-to-speech API available now in Gemini API and AI Studio — 200+ audio tags for directing delivery ([slowly, with gravity]), 70-language support, SynthID watermarking; free tier available

name: HuggingFace + GitHub Copilot Chat
summary: open-source models now plug into GitHub Copilot Chat in VS Code — swap in Qwen, Llama, or any HF model as your in-editor AI assistant without leaving your IDE

name: Claude Managed Agents + ant CLI
summary: public beta for building and deploying Claude agents — sandboxed execution, checkpointing, scoped permissions, SSE streaming; new ant CLI lets you ship agents from the terminal

name: Claude Sonnet 4.6
summary: major upgrade to the everyday workhorse model — faster coding, better agent planning, computer use improvements, and 1M token context window in beta

name: Qwen 3
summary: two Apache 2.0 releases: 72B dense and 235B MoE — both free to run locally, rival GPT-class performance for coding and reasoning without cloud costs

name: Llama 4 Scout + Maverick
summary: Meta's first natively multimodal open models — Scout (17B active/109B total MoE, runs on a single H100) and Maverick (17B active/400B total, beats GPT-4o on most benchmarks); video and image understanding, Apache 2.0

name: GPT-4o Image Generation
summary: native image generation baked directly into GPT-4o — no separate DALL-E call; edit existing images in chat, handles text in images accurately, remembers visual context from earlier in the conversation

name: Gemini 2.5 Pro
summary: Google's first explicitly 'thinking' model — beats o1 on AIME 2025 math competition, topped LMArena at launch; 1M context window, experimental release with strong coding and multimodal performance

name: Claude 3.7 Sonnet
summary: Anthropic's first hybrid reasoning model — toggle extended thinking for hard problems or skip it for speed; top scores on software engineering benchmarks; same price as 3.5 Sonnet

name: Grok 3
summary: xAI's first real flagship — trained on 200K H100s with Think and Big Brain reasoning modes; claimed top scores on AIME, GPQA, and LiveCodeBench; includes DeepSearch for real-time web synthesis

name: o3-mini
summary: OpenAI's first small reasoning model — three effort levels (low/medium/high); beats o1 on math and coding at a fraction of the cost; available in API and ChatGPT Plus from launch day

name: DeepSeek R1
summary: open-weights reasoning model that matched o1 at roughly 3% of the training cost — MIT licensed, 671B MoE with 37B active params; distilled variants down to 1.5B on HuggingFace; a turning point for open reasoning models

name: DeepSeek V3
summary: 671B MoE base model trained on 14.8T tokens using only 2048 H800 GPUs — MIT licensed, rivals GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 on benchmarks; a major open-source milestone from a Chinese lab operating on a fraction of the budget

name: Gemini 2.0 Flash
summary: Google's next-gen fast model — experimental launch with native multimodal output (text, audio, and image generation in one call), real-time streaming, and 1M context window; free to use in AI Studio

name: Claude 3.5 Haiku
summary: fastest Claude with a big jump — matches Claude 3 Opus on many benchmarks at Haiku-tier pricing; 200K context; computer use support; ideal for high-volume agentic pipelines where cost and speed matter

name: Claude 3.5 Sonnet (new) + Computer Use
summary: upgraded Sonnet with 50% better coding plus computer use API in public beta — Claude can see your screen, move the mouse, and type; the first general-purpose model with accessible GUI control

name: Llama 3.2
summary: Meta's first multimodal open-weights release — 11B and 90B vision models plus 1B/3B text models built for on-device use; Apache 2.0; marks vision understanding as table stakes for open models

name: o1-preview
summary: OpenAI's first public reasoning model — 'thinks before it answers' with a visible chain-of-thought; dominates math olympiad problems and PhD-level science questions; signals a shift from scaling training data to scaling inference compute
